Sánchez de la Sierra /ˈsæntʃɛz de la siˈɛrə/ 2020，韦格尔Weigel /ˈwaɪɡəl/ 2020）所建议的？
作者在第3节（第3586–3590页）呈现了一个两城市、两时期的空间均衡模型，以形式化国家建设的动态逻辑。该模型不仅是说明性的；它产生了一个精确的命题（命题1），其预测可以对照数据进行检验。
参与者和设定：有两座城市，c（省会）和r（竞争候选城市）。有两个时期，t ∈ {1, 2}。在第1期开始时，两座城市完全相同，除了c获得外生正向强制能力冲击Δ：C_{c,1} = C_{r,1} + Δ。有一个中央政府和一群流动公民N（总人口固定）。
生产：私人物品线性生产：Q_{j,t} = A_{j,t} × N_{j,t}，其中生产率A_{j,t} = A × (G_{j,t})^{β₁}取决于地方公共物品G_{j,t}。私人部门是竞争性的，因此工资等于边际产出：w_{j,t} = A_{j,t}。

财政技术：征税需要强制能力。城市j在时间t的税收收入为T_{j,t} = C_{j,t} × τ_{j,t} × N_{j,t} × w_{j,t}，其中τ是税率。没有强制能力（C = 0），就无法征税。政府以三种方式分配收入：份额μ_{j,t}用于地方公共物品（G_{j,t} = μ_{j,t} × T_{j,t}），份额ν_{j,t}用于全局公共物品（G̅_t由两座城市的资源通过柯布-道格拉斯技术组合生产，省会的贡献更有效率：α_c > α_r），份额（1 − μ_{j,t} − ν_{j,t}）用于强制能力投资。
强制能力动态：强制能力是随投资增长的存量：C_{j,t} = C_{j,t-1} × (B_{j,t-1})^σ，其中B_{j,t} = (1 − μ_{j,t} − ν_{j,t}) × T_{j,t}是分配给强制的预算。参数σ控制强制投资的回报。
公民偏好：城市j中的公民i最大化效用：U_{j}^{t}(i) = (Q_{j}^{t})^{γ₁} × (G_{j}^{t})^{β₂} × (G̅^{t})^{β₃} × exp(ε_{j}^{t}(i))，其中ε是个体偏好冲击（第一类极值分布）。公民每期完全流动，但基于当前（而非未来）条件做出选址决定。
政府目标：政府最大化公民对数间接效用的加权贴现和：ξ × V_{c,1} + (1−ξ) × V_{r,1} + δ × [ξ × V_{c,2} + (1−ξ) × V_{r,2}]，其中ξ是对省会福利的权重，δ是贴现因子。

关键结果（命题1）：在均衡中：（1a）在第1期，省会的税率严格更高。（1b）如果Δ < Δ̄（冲击不太大），c的地方公共物品更少、生产率更低、工资更低，人口流出c。（1c）在第2期，强制能力差距扩大了（因为两座城市分配给强制的预算份额相同，但c的总预算更大，所以强制的绝对投资更高）。（1d）在第2期，c提供了更多地方公共物品（在β₃不太大的条件下），生产率和工资更高，人口向c迁移。经济直觉是：在短期内，政府利用c的强制优势榨取更多资源并进一步积累强制存量，代价是较低的地方便利。在长期，积累的强制优势产生足够的收入来资助吸引工人和企业的地方公共物品。

en: “The Rise of Fiscal Capacity: Administration and State Consolidation in the Holy Roman Empire”
zh: 《财政能力的兴起：神圣罗马帝国的行政管理与国家整合》

en: Davide Cantoni /dɑːˈviːdeɪ kɑːnˈtoʊni/ (Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München /ˈmʏnçən/), Cathrin Mohr /ˈkæθrɪn moːɐ/ (Universität Bonn /bɒn/), and Matthias Weigand /mɑˈtiːɑs ˈvaɪɡɑnt/ (Harvard University).
zh: 达维德·坎托尼 /dɑːˈviːdeɪ kɑːnˈtoʊni/（路德维希-马克西米利安慕尼黑大学 /ˈmʏnçən/）、卡特琳·莫尔 /ˈkæθrɪn moːɐ/（波恩大学 /bɒn/）和马蒂亚斯·魏甘德 /mɑˈtiːɑs ˈvaɪɡɑnt/（哈佛大学）。

en: 2024, Econometrica, Vol. 92, No. 5, pp. 1439–1472.
zh: 2024年，发表于《计量经济学》(Econometrica) 第92卷第5期，第1439–1472页。

en: The paper examines three main dependent variables, all capturing different dimensions of state consolidation in the Holy Roman Empire /ˌhoʊli ˈroʊmən ˈɛmpaɪər/. First, territorial survival: whether a territory vanishes in a given year, coded as a binary indicator, disaggregated by cause — conflict/conquest, purchase, or dynastic extinction (p. 1451–1452). Second, territorial size: measured as the natural logarithm of the number of cities a territory rules, further decomposed into cities it rules alone (single ruler), uncontested cities, and total cities (p. 1454–1455). Third, territorial compactness: a measure of how cohesive and rounded a territory’s shape is, defined as the ratio of internal borders (shared between cities under the same ruler) to total borders of all city polygons in a territory, measured at both the territory level and the city level (p. 1456). These three outcomes — survival, size, and compactness — together constitute the authors’ operationalization of the overarching concept of “state consolidation” in the early modern period.
zh: 本文考察了三个主要因变量，它们分别刻画了神圣罗马帝国 /ˌhoʊli ˈroʊmən ˈɛmpaɪər/ 中国家整合（state consolidation）的不同维度。第一，领地存续（territorial survival）：某一领地是否在特定年份消亡，编码为二元指标，并按原因分解为冲突/征服、购买和王朝绝嗣三类（第1451–1452页）。第二，领地规模（territorial size）：用领地所统治城市数量的自然对数衡量，进一步区分为独治城市、无争议城市和全部城市（第1454–1455页）。第三，领地紧凑度（territorial compactness）：衡量领地形状的连贯性和圆整度，定义为内部边界（同一领主所辖城市之间的共享边界）与领地内全部城市多边形边界总长的比值，分别在领地层面和城市层面进行度量（第1456页）。这三项结果——存续、规模和紧凑度——共同构成了作者对近代早期“国家整合”这一上位概念的操作化衡量。

en: The main independent variable is fiscal centralization, operationalized as the introduction of a permanent, centralized fiscal administration—specifically, a Chamber (Hofkammer or Rentkammer)—within a territory. In the main panel regressions, this is coded as an absorbing binary indicator (Treated) that switches from 0 to 1 in the year a territory introduces such a Chamber and remains 1 thereafter. The authors document Chamber adoption for 39 territories between the sixteenth and eighteenth centuries and, in the main outcome specifications, interact Treated with Decades Since Treatment to capture the fact that the effects of fiscal centralization unfold gradually over time rather than appearing as a one-off shock.

In the paper’s instrumental-variables strategy, the relevant source of exogenous variation is not Chamber adoption itself but variation in the Imperial tax pressure faced by a territory. More precisely, the authors exploit the interaction between a territory’s fixed contribution share in the Imperial Register (Reichsmatrikel) of 1521 and the quasi-random timing and size of aggregate Imperial tax levies. Because Chamber adoption is an absorbing treatment, the IV is constructed as the maximum Imperial tax contribution a territory has faced up to year t, rather than simply the contemporaneous interaction term. This instrument is used to identify the causal effect of fiscal centralization on consolidation outcomes for the subset of territories whose adoption decisions were shifted by Imperial tax pressure.
zh: 主要自变量是财政集权化（fiscal centralization），其操作化方式是一个领地是否建立了永久性的、集中化的财政管理机构——具体而言，即设立了 财政室（Hofkammer 或 Rentkammer）。在正文的主回归中，这一变量被编码为一个吸收态二元指标（Treated）：在领地建立财政室的年份从 0 变为 1，此后保持为 1。作者记录了 16 世纪到 18 世纪之间 39 个领地引入财政室的时间；在主要结果方程中，又将 Treated 与 Decades Since Treatment 交互，以刻画财政集权化的影响并非一次性冲击，而是会随时间逐步展开。

在论文的工具变量识别策略中，真正提供外生变异的并不是财政室建立本身，而是一个领地所面临的帝国税压力。更准确地说，作者利用的是：领地在 1521 年帝国税册（Reichsmatrikel） 中的固定分摊份额，与帝国税总额在时间上和规模上的准随机波动之间的交互。由于财政室建立是一个吸收态处理，作者在 IV 中进一步将工具变量构造为：截至 t 年为止，该领地所面临过的最大帝国税贡献，而不是简单的当期交互项。这个 IV 用来识别财政集权化对国家整合结果的因果效应，但其识别对象是那些会被帝国税压力推动进入 reform 的领地。

en: The paper does not study the rise of the modern nation-state in the full Weberian sense. Rather, it analyzes the territorial consolidation of princely polities within the Holy Roman Empire in the early modern period. The relevant political units are the Empire’s constituent territories, whose rulers increasingly exercised sovereignty over the administrative and fiscal organization of their lands. In this setting, “state building” is best understood as the transformation from more personalized, fragmented, and overlapping forms of rule toward more durable, territorially bounded, and bureaucratically organized rule. The paper therefore focuses on territorial state consolidation, not on the emergence of a fully modern centralized nation-state with standardized citizenship, uniform law, and direct administration over a national population.

Empirically, the authors capture this consolidation through three principal outcome dimensions: survival, size, and compactness. Survival refers to whether a territory continues to exist rather than vanishing through conflict/conquest, purchase, or dynastic extinction. Size is measured by the number of cities a territory controls, with distinctions between cities ruled alone, uncontested cities, and all cities. Compactness captures how territorially cohesive a polity becomes, that is, whether its holdings are increasingly contiguous rather than scattered across enclaves and exclaves. These are the paper’s main observable indicators of consolidation, and the authors explicitly motivate them as the major aspects of territorial “enlargement” and survival in the early modern imperial setting.

Crucially, the paper treats fiscal capacity in a relatively narrow and historically specific sense. It is not defined as the ability to impose broad-based new taxation through representative bargaining. Instead, it refers primarily to the administrative capacity to collect, organize, monitor, and spend existing demesne-based revenues more efficiently. The Chamber is the operational marker of this capacity because it introduced centralized bookkeeping, systematic audits, and a specialized collegial office that concentrated fiscal knowledge. In theoretical terms, the paper draws less on a generic “war makes states” narrative than on an organizational account in which a new bureaucratic layer reduces fiscal complexity and improves the ruler’s ability to use preexisting revenue sources.
zh: 这篇论文研究的并不是完整韦伯意义上的现代民族国家兴起，而是近代早期神圣罗马帝国内部亲王领地的 territorial consolidation。作者关注的政治单位是帝国的组成性领地；随着时间推移，这些领地的统治者越来越多地掌握其土地的行政与财政组织权。在这样的背景下，论文中的 “state building” 更适合理解为：从更具人格性、碎片化、管辖重叠的统治形式，转向更持久、更具领土边界性、也更官僚化的统治形式。因此，本文讨论的首先是领地性的国家整合，而不是一个已经具备统一公民身份、统一法制与直接国民治理能力的现代 nation-state 的形成。
在经验层面，作者主要通过三个结果维度来把握这种整合：存续、规模和紧凑度。存续指领地是否继续存在，而不是因冲突/征服、购买或王朝绝嗣而消失；规模指领地控制城市的数量，并区分独治城市、无争议城市和全部城市；紧凑度则刻画一个 polity 的领土是否越来越连片，而非散落成大量飞地和孤立据点。这三项并不是对 “state” 概念的穷尽性定义，
而是论文用来观察 early modern imperial setting 中 territorial consolidation 的主要可观测指标；作者也明确将 survival 与 territorial “enlargement”
视为这一历史过程的关键面向。

关键在于，论文对财政能力的理解是狭义而历史特定的。它不是通过代议制协商来征收广泛新税的能力，而主要是对既有领地收入——即 demesne-based revenues——进行更有效率的征收、组织、监控与支出的行政能力。财政室之所以成为财政能力建设的操作性指标，正是因为它引入了集中化簿记、系统性审计，以及一个专门汇聚财政知识的合议制常设机构。就理论取向而言，本文并不只是复述一个一般性的 “war makes states” 故事，而是在组织理论意义上说明：新的官僚层级如何降低财政复杂性，并提高统治者利用既有收入来源的能力。

en: The core argument is that the development of fiscal capacity through permanent, centralized administrations (Chambers) was a crucial institutional innovation that drove state consolidation in the Holy Roman Empire. The argument unfolds in two steps.

First, regarding the origins of fiscal centralization: rulers faced a cost-benefit tradeoff when deciding whether to adopt a Chamber. The benefit was increased efficiency in extracting revenues from existing demesnes (not from new taxes); the cost was the fixed expense of establishing permanent offices and employing professional bureaucrats. The key exogenous driver that tipped this tradeoff was the imposition of Imperial taxes (Reichstürkenhilfe): at irregular intervals, the Imperial Diet required territorial rulers to process and remit war taxes (primarily to fund campaigns against the Ottoman /ˈɒtəmən/ Empire). While rulers could pass these taxes through to their subjects costlessly, having a sophisticated fiscal bureaucracy allowed them to retain a surplus from more efficient processing. The larger the Imperial tax request, the greater the incentive to adopt a Chamber. The quasi-random timing and size of these Imperial tax levies, combined with each territory’s fixed share in the Imperial Register of 1521, provided an exogenous, territory-specific fiscal demand shock that predicted Chamber adoption (p. 1441, 1446, 1449–1451).

Second, regarding the consequences: once a Chamber was established, territories experienced three interrelated consolidation outcomes. They were more likely to survive (specifically, less likely to vanish through conflict or purchase, though not through dynastic extinction, which is treated as exogenous). They grew larger, controlling more cities over time. And they became more compact, achieving more cohesive territorial shapes. These effects unfolded gradually over decades rather than appearing as an immediate, one-off shock (p. 1440, 1451–1456).

The authors are careful to note that this is not a story about taxation and representation. In the Holy Roman Empire, the taxation-representation nexus was resolved in favor of rulers and absolutism, not parliamentarism. Chambers operated entirely within the sovereign’s private finances, separate from and independent of the Estates (territorial parliaments). Over time, Chambers grew to become the dominant administrative institution, progressively absorbing fiscal tasks that had been associated with the Estates, thereby tilting the consolidating states toward bureaucratic-absolutist governance (p. 1442, 1458, 1468–1469).
zh: 核心论点是：通过永久性、集中化行政机构（财政室）发展起来的财政能力，是推动神圣罗马帝国国家整合的关键制度创新。论证分为两步展开。

第一，关于财政集权化的起源：统治者在决定是否设立财政室时面临成本—收益权衡。收益是提高对现有领地收入（而非新税）的征收效率；成本是建立常设机构和雇佣专业官僚的固定支出。使这一权衡发生倾斜的关键外生驱动因素是帝国税（Reichstürkenhilfe）的征收：帝国议会不定期地要求各领地统治者处理和上缴战争税款（主要用于资助对抗奥斯曼帝国 /ˈɒtəmən/ 的战役）。虽然统治者可以将这些税赋无成本地转嫁给臣民，但拥有精密的财政官僚机构使他们能够从更高效的税赋处理中截留盈余。帝国税请求越大，设立财政室的激励就越强。帝国税征收的准随机时间和规模，结合各领地在1521年帝国税册中的固定份额，为财政室的设立提供了一个外生的、因领地而异的财政需求冲击，有效预测了财政室的建立（第1441、1446、1449–1451页）。
第二，关于后果：财政室一旦建立，领地就经历了三个相互关联的整合结果。它们更有可能存续（具体而言，因冲突或购买而消亡的概率显著下降，但因王朝绝嗣而消亡的概率不受影响——后者被视为外生的）。它们的规模随时间逐步扩大，控制更多的城市。它们的形状也变得更加紧凑，实现了更连片的领土版图。这些效应是在数十年的过程中逐渐展开的，而非作为一次性冲击即时显现（第1440、1451–1456页）。
作者审慎地指出，这并非一个关于征税与代议制关系的故事。在神圣罗马帝国，征税—代议制的关系是以有利于统治者和专制主义的方式解决的，而非议会主义。财政室完全在领主的私人财政范围内运作，独立于等级会议（领地议会）。随着时间推移，财政室成长为最主要的行政机构，逐步吸收了原本与等级会议相关联的财政职能，从而将正在整合中的国家推向官僚—专制主义治理（第1442、1458、1468–1469页）。

en: The authors identify four concrete mechanisms through which fiscal centralization translated into improved consolidation outcomes.

1. Increased revenues (p. 1460–1461). Historical revenue data for Hesse /hɛsə/ and Albertine Saxony /ˈælbɜːtiːn ˈsæksəni/ show that revenues increased substantially following the introduction of a Chamber. In Hesse, revenues jumped by over 36,000 guilders in the decade after centralization. In Albertine Saxony, drink excise tax revenues rose from an average of 21,000 guilders to approximately 26,000 guilders per year after the 1524 reform. Crucially, these increases came from more efficient exploitation of existing revenue sources (demesnes), not from new taxes, consistent with the Chamber’s mandate to improve administrative efficiency rather than expand the tax base.

2. Reduced reliance on pawning (p. 1461–1462). Before Chambers, rulers frequently pawned cities and land holdings to local nobles in exchange for short-term cash — an inefficient and self-defeating practice that eroded the revenue base. After fiscal centralization, the probability of a city being pawned dropped sharply and immediately (as shown in event-study graphs), indicating that improved fiscal administration allowed rulers to generate revenue through regular channels rather than selling off assets. In Hesse, the Chamber redeemed 28 local offices and spent 100,000 guilders redeeming pawns within a decade; in Albertine Saxony, the largest post-reform expenditure (46,190 guilders) went to pawn redemption.

3. Military investment and defensive success (p. 1463–1464). Following fiscal centralization, cities in treated territories experienced a significant increase in military construction (castles, arsenals, fortifications) — about 0.04 additional buildings per century, against a baseline of 0.051 per century. This near-doubling of military infrastructure investment was not driven by increased attacks or changes in the external threat environment. Importantly, when cities in centralized territories were attacked, the probability of losing the city to another ruler was reduced to nearly zero (the interaction of Attack × Treated almost exactly offsets the main effect of Attack on rule changes due to violence), while there was no such defensive advantage for rule changes due to purchase or extinction. This confirms that military investment following fiscal centralization substantially increased defensive capabilities.

4. Strategic marriage success (p. 1464–1466). Marriages were a primary tool of territorial expansion through inheritance claims. They were also expensive: dowries and wedding ceremonies required large sums from the ruler’s treasury, which Chambers helped finance. The authors measure marriage success by the change in a daughter’s dynastic network connectedness resulting from her marriage — specifically, the number of territorial rulers and ruled cities within three degrees of family separation. After fiscal centralization, rulers’ daughters gained 29% more connected rulers and 70% more connected land holdings through their marriages, reflecting substantially improved outcomes of marriage diplomacy.

The authors further conduct a mediation analysis (p. 1466–1468) in which they interact the treatment with features that amplify specific mechanisms. Territories whose Chamber ordinance explicitly included a construction mandate showed larger effects on size and compactness. Territories where the ruler had marriageable daughters showed additional consolidation gains. While statistical power is limited (given the small number of territories with these features), the interaction coefficients point in the expected directions and are quantitatively meaningful.
zh: 1. 收入增加（第1460–1461页）。 黑森 /hɛsə/ 和阿尔贝廷萨克森 /ˈælbɜːtiːn ˈsæksəni/ 的历史收入数据显示，财政室设立后收入大幅增长。在黑森，集权化后十年内收入增加了超过36,000基尔登。在阿尔贝廷萨克森，饮品消费税收入从平均每年21,000基尔登上升至1524年改革后的约26,000基尔登。关键在于，这些增长来自对现有收入来源（领地产业）更有效率的开发，而非新税种，这与财政室旨在提高行政效率而非扩大税基的使命相一致。

2. 减少对城市质押的依赖（第1461–1462页）。 在财政室建立之前，统治者经常将城市和土地质押给地方贵族以换取短期现金——这是一种低效且自我削弱的做法，会侵蚀收入基础。财政集权化之后，城市被质押的概率急剧且立即下降（如事件研究图所示），表明改进的财政管理使统治者能够通过常规渠道获取收入，而不必出售资产。在黑森，财政室在十年内赎回了28个地方机构，并花费100,000基尔登赎回质押；在阿尔贝廷萨克森，改革后最大的一笔支出（46,190基尔登）用于赎回质押。

3. 军事投资与防御成功（第1463–1464页）。 财政集权化后，实验组领地中的城市在军事建筑（城堡、军械库、防御工事）方面出现了显著增长——每世纪约增加0.04座建筑，而基线水平为每世纪0.051座。这种几近翻倍的军事基础设施投资并非由攻击增多或外部威胁环境变化所驱动。重要的是，当集权化领地的城市确实遭到攻击时，城市被夺走的概率几乎降至零（Attack × Treated的交互效应几乎完全抵消了Attack对因暴力导致统治者变更的主效应），而对于因购买或绝嗣导致的统治者变更则无此防御优势。这证实了财政集权化后的军事投资大幅提高了防御能力。

4. 战略婚姻成功（第1464–1466页）。 婚姻是通过继承权主张进行领土扩张的主要工具。婚姻也是昂贵的：嫁妆和婚礼仪式需要从统治者的国库中支出大笔资金，而财政室有助于为此提供资金。作者通过一位女儿的婚姻所带来的王朝网络联通性变化来度量婚姻成功——具体而言，即婚后三度家族分隔范围内的领地统治者数量和被统治城市数量。财政集权化后，统治者的女儿通过婚姻增加了29%的关联统治者和70%的关联领地，反映出婚姻外交成果的大幅改善。
作者还进行了中介分析（第1466–1468页），将处理变量与放大特定机制的特征进行交互。那些财政室条例中明确包含建筑任务的领地，在规模和紧凑度上表现出更大的效应。统治者拥有适婚女儿的领地表现出额外的整合收益。虽然统计检验力有限（因为具有这些特征的领地数量较少），但交互系数的方向符合预期，且在数量上具有实质意义。

en: The paper is built around a major, novel data collection effort, the scale of which is itself a significant contribution.

Core dataset — cities and territories (p. 1440, 1446–1447). The authors construct a panel linking each of the 2,382 cities in the Deutsches Städtebuch /ˈdɔʏtʃəs ˈʃtɛːtəbuːx/ (Keyser et al., 1939–2003), a comprehensive encyclopedia of cities in Central Europe, to one or multiple ruling dynasties for every year between 1400 and 1789. For each city-year, they record the kind of rule, the hierarchy of rulers (if multiple), and reasons for any rule changes. From this, they aggregate to the territory level, identifying 636 distinct territorial entities, 102,825 territory-year observations, and 15,568 rule transitions. Some analyses are conducted at the city-year level (826,408 observations) (p. 1440, 1447).

Fiscal centralization data (p. 1448). They construct a novel dataset on the dates of Chamber introduction for 39 territories by supplementing and cross-verifying the comprehensive administrative history handbook by Jeserich /ˈjeːzərɪç/, Pohl /poːl/, and von Unruh /fɔn ˈʊnruː/ (1983) with a large number of publications on fiscal and regional history. There is considerable variation in adoption timing: Württemberg /ˈvʏrtəmbɛrk/ and Albertine Saxony were first (early 16th century), while Schaumburg-Lippe /ˈʃaʊmbʊrk ˈlɪpə/, Paderborn /ˈpɑːdɐbɔrn/, and Reuß-Greiz /rɔʏs ɡraɪts/ did not adopt until the 18th century.

Dynastic network data (p. 1447). The authors identify 2,799 rulers of secular territories in an extensive kinship and marriage network of over 133,000 members of noble families sourced from Marek /ˈmɑːrɛk/ (2018). For each individual, dates of birth, death, and marriage, as well as full offspring and marriage links, are known. This allows the calculation of network-based measures of dynastic connectedness.

Spatial data (p. 1447). Lacking detailed year-by-year border maps, the authors approximate territorial shape by drawing Thiessen /ˈtiːsən/ (Voronoi /vɒrɒˈnɔɪ/) polygons around city center-points and aggregating polygons belonging to the same ruler in a given year. This enables the computation of compactness measures. They also construct modified polygons accounting for terrain ruggedness and river velocity as robustness checks.

Imperial tax data (p. 1448). Territories are mapped to the Imperial Register (Reichsmatrikel) of 1521, which assigned a fixed share of the Imperial tax burden to each territory. The timing and total size of Imperial tax levies — raised 30 times between 1522 and 1740 — are collected from historical sources.

Additional covariates (p. 1448–1449). The dataset is enriched with geographic variables (terrain ruggedness, distance to navigable water, agricultural suitability, mining activity), economic indicators (construction events, market grants from the Deutsches Städtebuch), conflict data (attacks to cities), institutional data (advisory councils, Estates/territorial diets), and neighborhood characteristics (military construction in foreign neighboring cities, share of fiscally centralized neighbors).

Geographic and temporal coverage: The analysis covers all territories and cities within the Holy Roman Empire (approximating the borders of Germany in 1937), from 1400 to 1789, excluding territories outside the Empire or largely outside the area of analysis (such as Bohemia /boʊˈhiːmiə/ and the Further Austrian territories of the Habsburgs /ˈhæpsbɜːrɡ/) (p. 1447).
zh: 本文建立在一项大规模的、原创性的数据采集工作之上，其数据规模本身即为重要贡献。

核心数据集——城市与领地（第1440、1446–1447页）。 作者构建了一个面板数据集，将《德国城市志》（Deutsches Städtebuch /ˈdɔʏtʃəs ˈʃtɛːtəbuːx/，Keyser等人，1939–2003）中全部2,382座中欧城市逐一链接到1400年至1789年间每一年度的一个或多个统治王朝。对每个城市-年度观测值，记录统治类型、统治者层级（如有多个统治者）以及任何统治者变更的原因。从中汇总到领地层面，识别出636个不同的领地实体、102,825个领地-年度观测值和15,568次统治者交接。部分分析在城市-年度层面进行（826,408个观测值）（第1440、1447页）。

财政集权化数据（第1448页）。 作者通过补充和交叉核验耶泽里希 /ˈjeːzərɪç/、波尔 /poːl/ 和冯·温鲁 /fɔn ˈʊnruː/（1983）的综合行政史手册以及大量财政史和地方史文献，构建了一个关于39个领地引入财政室日期的原创数据集。采纳时间差异很大：符腾堡 /ˈvʏrtəmbɛrk/ 和阿尔贝廷萨克森最早（16世纪初），而绍姆堡-利珀 /ˈʃaʊmbʊrk ˈlɪpə/、帕德博恩 /ˈpɑːdɐbɔrn/ 和罗伊斯-格赖茨 /rɔʏs ɡraɪts/ 直到18世纪才采纳。
王朝网络数据（第1447页）。 作者从马雷克 /ˈmɑːrɛk/（2018）的数据中识别出2,799名世俗领地的统治者，这些统治者位于一个包含超过133,000名贵族成员的广泛亲属和婚姻网络中。对每个个体，已知出生、死亡和婚姻日期，以及完整的后代和婚姻关系。这使得计算基于网络的王朝联通性度量成为可能。
空间数据（第1447页）。 由于缺乏详细的逐年边界地图，作者通过在城市中心点周围绘制泰森 /ˈtiːsən/（沃罗诺伊 /vɒrɒˈnɔɪ/）多边形，并将同一统治者在给定年份的多边形汇总，来近似领地形状。这使紧凑度度量的计算成为可能。作为稳健性检验，他们还构建了考虑地形崎岖度和河流流速的修正多边形。
帝国税数据（第1448页）。 领地被映射至1521年帝国税册（Reichsmatrikel），该税册为每个领地分配了固定的帝国税负担份额。帝国税征收的时间和总额——在1522年至1740年间共征收了30次——从历史文献中采集。
其他控制变量（第1448–1449页）。 数据集还纳入了地理变量（地形崎岖度、距通航水道距离、农业适宜性、采矿活动）、经济指标（建筑事件、来自《德国城市志》的市场特许权）、冲突数据（城市遭受攻击）、制度数据（顾问委员会、等级会议/领地议会）以及邻域特征（外国邻近城市的军事建设、财政集权化邻域的比例）。
地理和时间覆盖范围： 分析涵盖神圣罗马帝国内（大致对应1937年德国边界内）的全部领地和城市，时间跨度为1400年至1789年，排除了帝国之外或主要位于分析区域之外的领地（如波西米亚 /boʊˈhiːmiə/ 和哈布斯堡王朝 /ˈhæpsbɜːrɡ/ 的远奥地利领地）（第1447页）。

en: The paper employs a range of econometric methods, all built around a staggered difference-in-differences design with a rich panel dataset.

1. Predicting Chamber adoption — hazard model (Section 4, p. 1449–1451). The authors estimate a linear probability model at the territory-decade level to identify predictors of fiscal centralization. The dependent variable is a binary indicator for Chamber adoption in a given decade; once a territory adopts, it exits the sample (reflecting the absorbing state). The regression includes territory and decade fixed effects. Covariates are grouped into geographic, internal composition, commercial/economic, external military pressure, and Imperial tax contribution variables. The key finding is that the interaction of the territory’s fixed contribution share with the logged total Imperial tax levy (Roman Months) is the only consistently significant predictor across all specifications, including Cox hazard rate regressions.

2. Main effects — two-way fixed effects (TWFE) regressions (Section 5, p. 1451–1457). For each of the three consolidation outcomes (vanishing, size, compactness), the authors estimate panel regressions at the territory-year or city-year level with territory (and/or city) and year fixed effects. The treatment variable Treated is interacted with Decades Since Treatment to capture the gradually unfolding effect. Standard errors are clustered at the territory level throughout.

3. Event studies (p. 1453, 1455–1457). The authors estimate event-study specifications that interact the treatment indicator with relative decade dummies (for decades before and after treatment). These serve two purposes: demonstrating the absence of immediate pre-trends (supporting the parallel trends assumption) and visualizing the dynamic trajectory of treatment effects. For the vanishing outcome, pre-treatment coefficients cannot be estimated because sample inclusion is conditional on survival to the treatment date.

4. Robustness — confounders (p. 1457–1458). The authors control for a range of time-varying, territory-specific potential confounders: the external military threat environment, concurrent institutional changes (advisory councils), and the role of Estates/parliaments. They show that Chamber ordinances were issued separately from council ordinances and that accounting for Estates does not alter results. They emphasize that in the Holy Roman Empire, unlike England or France, the taxation-representation nexus was resolved in favor of absolutism.

5. Robustness — matching (p. 1459). The authors implement nearest neighbor matching, full matching, and restrict to territories in the 90th percentile of adoption probability (predicted from a cross-section Probit on 1500 characteristics). They repeat these matching strategies for territory size in 1500 and for the Imperial Register contribution share. Results are robust across all approaches.

6. Robustness — intensive margin (p. 1459). Restricting to only the 39 territories that eventually adopt a Chamber yields qualitatively and quantitatively similar results, addressing concerns about comparisons between fundamentally different types of territories and potential SUTVA violations/spillovers.

7. Instrumental variables (p. 1459–1460). The authors construct a shift-share IV: the instrument is the maximum cumulative Imperial tax contribution a territory has faced up to year t (the interaction of the fixed Reichsmatrikel share and the quasi-randomly varying total tax levy). Following Borusyak /bɒˈruːʃæk/, Hull /hʌl/, and Jaravel /ˈʒɑːrɑːvɛl/ (2022), the identifying assumption is that the temporal shocks to the overall tax level are orthogonal, even if the territory-specific share may be endogenously determined. The IV estimates are qualitatively similar to OLS but larger in magnitude, consistent with higher returns to Chamber adoption for compliers. The authors note two limitations: the IV only recovers a LATE (for compliers induced by Imperial tax pressure), and because treatment is binary/absorbing while the IV is continuous, the IV cannot estimate the dynamic time-since-treatment effects or event studies.

8. Robustness — heterogeneous treatment effects (p. 1460). The authors address concerns from the recent econometrics literature (Sun and Abraham 2021; De Chaisemartin and d'Haultfœuille 2020) about bias in TWFE with staggered adoption. They show robustness to excluding individual territories (ruling out that results are driven by outliers like Prussia /ˈprʌʃə/) and employ the De Chaisemartin and d'Haultfœuille (2024) estimator.

Key identifying assumptions: (a) Conditional on territory and year fixed effects, there are no unobserved, time-varying, territory-specific confounders that coincide with Chamber adoption and independently drive consolidation. (b) The parallel trends assumption: absent treatment, treated and control territories would have followed similar trajectories. The event studies support this by showing no immediate pre-trends, though the authors acknowledge that some results are not robust to the inclusion of linear territory trends. (c) For the IV: the exclusion restriction requires that Imperial tax levies affect consolidation outcomes only through their effect on Chamber adoption (i.e., not through direct war exposure, which the authors argue is satisfied since no territory in their analysis was directly affected by Ottoman campaigns, p. 1441). (d) Monotonicity: higher Imperial tax contributions weakly increase the probability of Chamber adoption. (e) SUTVA: the treatment effect for one territory does not depend on the treatment status of other territories, which is partially addressed by the intensive-margin analysis.

Although the authors do not present a fully formalized mathematical model in the main text (a formal treatment is in Supplemental Appendix, Section C, p. 1449), they describe a clear stylized framework for the adoption decision. The key elements are:
A territory’s ruler faces fiscal demands that vary over time. These demands come from two sources: internal needs (court expenses, feuds, military costs) and external shocks (Imperial tax levies).

Without a Chamber, complex fiscal tasks cannot be handled efficiently even by the prince and his advisors. This creates an efficiency loss in raising and spending revenues from demesnes. The size of this efficiency loss depends on the complexity of the fiscal environment, proxied by geographic features (terrain ruggedness, dispersed land holdings) and the composition of demesne types.

Introducing a Chamber adds a specialized administrative layer to the territorial hierarchy (following Garicano 2000’s knowledge hierarchy model). This layer concentrates fiscal expertise, enabling the systematic auditing of local offices, centralized bookkeeping, and the tracking of revenue flows. For a given level of fiscal demands and demesne size, this narrows the efficiency gap and benefits princely finances.
Against these benefits stand the fixed costs of establishing the Chamber: setting up permanent offices and paying professional bureaucrats.

The key time-varying shock to fiscal demands comes from Imperial taxes: when the Imperial Diet requests a large tax, a territory with a Chamber can process it more efficiently and retain a surplus proportional to the size of the request. This surplus-retention incentive scales with both the territory’s fixed share in the Imperial Register and the total tax requested.

In each period, the ruler compares the static efficiency gains from a Chamber against its fixed costs. When a sufficiently large Imperial tax request pushes the benefits above the fixed cost threshold, the ruler adopts a Chamber. This explains why Chamber adoption clusters around Imperial Diet sessions that approved large tax levies (p. 1449–1451).
zh: 1. 预测财政室建立——风险模型（第4节，第1449–1451页）。 作者在领地-十年层面估计了一个线性概率模型，以识别财政集权化的预测因素。因变量为给定十年内是否建立财政室的二元指标；一旦领地采纳，即退出样本（反映吸收态特征）。回归包含领地和十年固定效应。协变量分为地理、内部构成、商业/经济、外部军事压力和帝国税贡献五组。核心发现是：领地固定贡献份额与帝国税总额（罗马月）对数的交互项是所有设定（包括Cox比例风险回归）中唯一始终显著的预测因素。
2. 主效应——双向固定效应（TWFE）回归（第5节，第1451–1457页）。 对于三个整合结果（消亡、规模、紧凑度）中的每一个，作者在领地-年度或城市-年度层面进行面板回归，包含领地（和/或城市）和年份固定效应。处理变量Treated与Decades Since Treatment交互，以捕捉效应逐步展开的特征。全文均在领地层面进行标准误聚类。
3. 事件研究（第1453、1455–1457页）。 作者估计了事件研究设定，将处理指标与相对十年虚拟变量（处理前后的各十年）交互。这有两个目的：证明不存在即时的处理前趋势（支持平行趋势假设），以及可视化处理效应的动态轨迹。对于消亡结果，由于样本的纳入以存续到处理年份为条件，处理前系数无法估计。
4. 稳健性——混淆因素（第1457–1458页）。 作者控制了一系列随时间变化的、领地特定的潜在混淆因素：外部军事威胁环境、同期的制度变革（顾问委员会）以及等级会议/议会的角色。他们表明财政室条例是独立于委员会条例发布的，且控制等级会议不影响结果。他们强调，与英格兰或法国不同，在神圣罗马帝国中，征税—代议制的关系是以有利于专制主义的方式解决的。
5. 稳健性——匹配（第1459页）。 作者实施了最近邻匹配、完全匹配，并限制在采纳概率第90百分位以上的领地（基于1500年特征的截面Probit模型预测）。他们还对1500年的领地规模和帝国税册贡献份额重复了这些匹配策略。所有方法下结果均稳健。
6. 稳健性——密集边际（第1459页）。 仅限于最终采纳财政室的39个领地进行分析，结果在定性和定量上相似，回应了关于根本不同类型领地之间比较以及潜在SUTVA违反/溢出效应的关切。

7. 工具变量（第1459–1460页）。 作者构建了一个份额-冲击（shift-share）工具变量：工具变量为一个领地截至t年所面临的最大累计帝国税贡献（帝国税册固定份额与准随机变化的税收总额的交互项）。依据博鲁沙克 /bɒˈruːʃæk/、赫尔 /hʌl/ 和贾拉韦尔 /ˈʒɑːrɑːvɛl/（2022），识别假设为：对总税额的时间冲击是正交的，即使领地特定份额可能是内生决定的。IV估计值在方向上与OLS一致，但幅度更大，与对遵从者（compliers）而言财政室采纳回报更高相一致。作者指出两个局限：IV仅恢复局部平均处理效应（LATE，针对受帝国税压力驱动进入处理的遵从者）；由于处理是二元/吸收态而IV是连续的，IV无法估计动态的处理后时间效应或事件研究。
8. 稳健性——异质性处理效应（第1460页）。 作者回应了近期计量经济学文献（Sun和Abraham 2021；De Chaisemartin和d'Haultfœuille 2020）关于交错采纳设计中TWFE偏误的关切。他们证明了排除单个领地后结果的稳健性（排除了普鲁士 /ˈprʌʃə/ 等异常值驱动结果的可能），并使用了
De Chaisemartin和d'Haultfœuille（2024）的估计量。

关键识别假设：（a）在控制领地和年份固定效应的条件下，不存在与财政室建立同时发生且独立驱动整合的、未观测到的、随时间变化的领地特定混淆因素。（b）平行趋势假设：在没有处理的情况下，处理组和对照组领地将遵循相似的轨迹。事件研究通过显示不存在即时处理前趋势来支持这一点，但作者承认某些结果对加入领地线性趋势不稳健。（c）对于IV：排他性约束要求帝国税征收仅通过其对财政室建立的影响来影响整合结果（即不通过直接的战争暴露，作者论证这一条件得到满足，因为其分析中没有领地直接受到奥斯曼战役的影响，第1441页）。（d）单调性：更高的帝国税贡献弱增加财政室建立的概率。（e）SUTVA：一个领地的处理效应不取决于其他领地的处理状态，这在一定程度上通过密集边际分析得到处理。
虽然作者在正文中没有提出一个完全形式化的数学模型（正式处理见补充附录C节，第1449页），但他们描述了一个清晰的程式化框架来解释采纳决策。关键要素如下：
领地统治者面临随时间变化的财政需求。这些需求来自两个来源：内部需要（宫廷开支、封建争斗、军事费用）和外部冲击（帝国税征收）。
在没有财政室的情况下，即使是亲王和他的顾问也无法有效处理复杂的财政任务。这导致了从领地产业中征收和支出收入时的效率损失。效率损失的大小取决于财政环境的复杂程度，以地理特征（地形崎岖度、分散的土地持有）和领地产业类型的构成为代理。
引入财政室为领地层级增添了一个专业化行政层（遵循Garicano 2000的知识层级模型）。该层级集中了财政专业知识，使地方办公室的系统化审计、集中化簿记和收入流跟踪成为可能。在给定的财政需求和领地规模下，这缩小了效率差距并有益于亲王的财政。
与这些收益相对的是建立财政室的固定成本：设立常设机构和支付专业官僚的费用。
财政需求的关键时变冲击来自帝国税：当帝国议会要求征收大额税款时，拥有财政室的领地能够更高效地处理税款并截留一个与请求规模成正比的盈余。这一盈余截留激励随领地在帝国税册中的固定份额和请求的税额总量而增大。
在每个时期，统治者比较财政室带来的静态效率收益与其固定成本。当一次足够大的帝国税请求使收益超过固定成本门槛时，统治者就采纳财政室。这解释了为何财政室的建立集中在批准大额税收的帝国议会会议前后（第1449–1451页）。

en: The paper makes several significant contributions that explain its publication in Econometrica, the field’s most prestigious journal.

1. Original, large-scale historical data (p. 1440, 1446–1448). The construction of a panel dataset linking 2,382 cities to their rulers for every year over nearly four centuries (1400–1789), encompassing 636 territories, 102,825 territory-year observations, and a kinship/marriage network of 133,000+ nobles, represents a data collection effort of exceptional scope. This enables the observation of all territories — not just ex post successful ones like Prussia or England — thereby overcoming the survivorship bias that Tilly (1975) identified as a fundamental problem in the study of European state formation.

2. Credibly establishing the causal effect of fiscal institutions on state consolidation (p. 1440–1441, 1457–1460). The paper provides the most rigorous quantitative evidence to date for the long-theorized but never well-documented link between fiscal capacity and state survival/growth. It does so through a battery of identification strategies: TWFE with event studies showing no pre-trends, extensive controls for time-varying confounders, multiple matching strategies, intensive-margin analysis, a shift-share IV exploiting the quasi-random Imperial tax shocks, and robust estimators for heterogeneous treatment effects in staggered designs.

3. Identifying mechanisms through which fiscal institutions drive consolidation (p. 1460–1468). The paper goes beyond a reduced-form finding by documenting four specific channels: revenues, pawn reduction, military investment, and marriage diplomacy. This granular mechanism analysis connects the institutional innovation to its ultimate effects through observable, intermediate outcomes.

4. Enriching the theory of the “bifurcation” between absolutism and parliamentarism (p. 1442, 1468–1469). By showing that in the Holy Roman Empire, fiscal capacity building occurred outside and at the expense of representative institutions (Estates), the paper adds an important dimension to the literature on the divergent paths of European state development — complementing the well-studied cases of England (parliamentary control) and France/Spain (Estates sidelined). The finding that Chambers, as fiscal bureaucracies, tilted states toward absolutism offers a concrete institutional mechanism for this divergence.

5. Bridging historical and contemporary research on fiscal capacity (p. 1442). The paper explicitly connects the early modern European experience to contemporary developing economies that feature similar information gaps and low fiscal capacity, making its findings relevant to a broader audience studying state building in fragile states.
zh: 1. 原创性的大规模历史数据（第1440、1446–1448页）。 构建了一个将2,382座城市与其统治者在近四个世纪（1400–1789）中每一年进行链接的面板数据集，涵盖636个领地、102,825个领地-年度观测值以及超过133,000名贵族的亲属/婚姻网络，代表了一项范围非凡的数据采集工作。这使得对所有领地——而非仅仅事后成功的如普鲁士或英格兰——的观测成为可能，从而克服了蒂利（1975）所指出的欧洲国家形成研究中的根本性生存者偏差问题。

2. 可信地建立了财政制度对国家整合的因果效应（第1440–1441、1457–1460页）。 本文为长期以来在理论上被假设但从未得到充分记录的财政能力与国家存续/增长之间的联系，提供了迄今为止最严格的定量证据。这通过一系列识别策略来实现：具有事件研究（显示无处理前趋势）的TWFE、对时变混淆因素的广泛控制、多种匹配策略、密集边际分析、利用准随机帝国税冲击的份额-冲击工具变量，以及针对交错设计中异质性处理效应的稳健估计量。
3. 识别财政制度驱动整合的机制（第1460–1468页）。 本文超越了简约式发现，记录了四个具体渠道：收入增加、质押减少、军事投资和婚姻外交。这种精细的机制分析将制度创新与其最终效应通过可观测的中间结果联系起来。
4. 丰富了专制主义与议会主义之间“分岔”的理论（第1442、1468–1469页）。 通过表明在神圣罗马帝国中，财政能力建设发生在代议制度（等级会议）之外且以其为代价，本文为欧洲国家发展分化路径的文献增添了一个重要维度——补充了已被充分研究的英格兰案例（议会控制）和法国/西班牙案例（等级会议被边缘化）。财政室作为财政官僚机构推动国家走向专制主义这一发现，为这种分化提供了一个具体的制度机制。
5. 连接历史与当代财政能力研究（第1442页）。 本文明确将近代早期欧洲的经验与当代发展中经济体——这些经济体具有类似的信息差距和低财政能力——联系起来，使其发现对研究脆弱国家国家建设的更广泛受众具有相关性。

en: Professional scholars and researchers would likely identify the following limitations:

1. The causal identification challenge of a slowly unfolding treatment. The authors themselves acknowledge (p. 1458) that because the effect of Chambers takes hold gradually over decades, it is difficult to disentangle the treatment effect from pre-existing differences in long-term trends. Indeed, they note that some results are not robust to the inclusion of linear territory-specific trends. This is a fundamental tension: the gradual nature of the effect is historically plausible and theoretically expected, but it weakens the canonical difference-in-differences identification strategy. A potential improvement might involve more aggressively exploiting the timing variation in Chamber adoption — for instance, through regression discontinuity designs around specific Imperial Diet sessions, or by developing more refined measures of the “dosage” of fiscal centralization rather than treating it as a binary, absorbing state.

2. Measurement of fiscal centralization as a binary variable. The Chamber adoption indicator is a coarse measure. Chambers varied in size, mandate, professionalization, and effectiveness across territories and over time. Treating all Chambers as identical likely masks substantial heterogeneity. Future research could attempt to code the “intensity” of fiscal centralization — for example, the number of officials, the scope of the mandate, or the frequency of audits — to capture variation in the quality of the institutional reform rather than just its presence.

3. Limited direct evidence on revenues. The revenue mechanism is supported by data from only two territories (Hesse and Albertine Saxony) (p. 1460–1461). While the pawning evidence provides a clever indirect test for the broader sample, the direct link between Chamber adoption and increased revenues remains descriptive and based on a very small number of cases. This is partly a data constraint, since systematic recordkeeping was itself a consequence of the Chamber. Scholars might recommend systematic archival efforts to gather revenue data for additional territories.

4. Potential reverse causality and selection. While the authors control for many confounders and employ an IV, the possibility remains that territories on an upward trajectory (due to unobserved ruler quality, geopolitical luck, or cultural factors) were both more likely to adopt a Chamber and to consolidate. The IV addresses this for compliers, but the LATE may not generalize to always-takers or never-takers. The larger IV estimates compared to OLS could reflect either higher returns for compliers or remaining endogeneity in the OLS specification.

5. The SUTVA concern. State consolidation in the Holy Roman Empire was inherently a competitive, zero-sum process: one territory’s gain in cities was another’s loss. The treatment effect of one territory’s Chamber adoption therefore likely depends on the treatment status of its neighbors and competitors. While the intensive-margin analysis (restricting to the 39 eventually-treated territories) partially addresses this, a full account of strategic interaction and spillovers would require a game-theoretic or spatial econometric framework.

6. Generalizability. The Holy Roman Empire is a unique institutional setting — a loose confederation of largely sovereign states with overlapping jurisdictions, elected emperors, and a particular legal/constitutional framework. Whether the findings generalize to state formation in other parts of Europe (France, England, Iberia), let alone to state building in contemporary developing countries, is not self-evident and would require careful comparative analysis.
zh: 1. 缓慢展开的处理效应带来的因果识别挑战。 作者自己承认（第1458页），由于财政室的效应在数十年间逐步展开，难以将处理效应与长期趋势的既有差异区分开来。事实上，他们指出某些结果在加入领地特定的线性趋势后不再稳健。这是一个根本性的张力：效应的渐进性在历史上是合理的且在理论上是预期的，但它削弱了经典双重差分识别策略的效力。潜在改进可能包括更积极地利用财政室建立的时间变异——例如，围绕特定帝国议会会议进行断点回归设计，或者开发更精细的财政集权化“剂量”度量，而非将其视为二元吸收态。
2. 财政集权化作为二元变量的度量。 财政室建立指标是一个粗糙的度量。不同领地和不同时期的财政室在规模、职权范围、专业化程度和有效性上存在差异。将所有财政室视为相同可能掩盖了实质性的异质性。未来研究可以尝试编码财政集权化的“强度”——例如，官员数量、职权范围或审计频率——以捕捉制度改革质量的变异，而非仅仅其存在与否。
3. 收入方面直接证据有限。 收入机制仅有来自两个领地（黑森和阿尔贝廷萨克森）的数据支持（第1460–1461页）。虽然质押证据为更广泛样本提供了巧妙的间接检验，但财政室建立与收入增加之间的直接联系仍然是描述性的，且基于极少数案例。这部分是数据约束，因为系统化的记录保存本身就是财政室的产物。学者可能建议对更多领地进行系统性的档案收集以获取收入数据。
4. 潜在的逆向因果与选择偏差。 尽管作者控制了许多混淆因素并使用了IV，仍然存在这样的可能性：处于上升轨道的领地（由于不可观测的统治者素质、地缘政治运气或文化因素）既更可能建立财政室，也更可能实现整合。IV针对遵从者处理了这一问题，但LATE可能无法推广至始终处理者或从不处理者。IV估计值大于OLS可能反映的是遵从者的更高回报，也可能是OLS设定中残留的内生性。
5. SUTVA问题。 神圣罗马帝国中的国家整合在本质上是一个竞争性的零和过程：一个领地城市的增加就是另一个领地的损失。因此，一个领地建立财政室的处理效应很可能取决于其邻居和竞争者的处理状态。虽然密集边际分析（限制在39个最终被处理的领地）部分地处理了这一问题，但对战略互动和溢出效应的完整解释将需要博弈论或空间计量经济学框架。
6. 可推广性。 神圣罗马帝国是一个独特的制度环境——一个由大量基本上拥有主权的国家组成的松散邦联，具有重叠的管辖权、选举制皇帝和特定的法律/宪制框架。这些发现是否可以推广至欧洲其他地区（法国、英格兰、伊比利亚）的国家形成，更不用说推广至当代发展中国家的国家建设，并非不言而喻，需要谨慎的比较分析。

en: 1. Strategic Interaction and the Spatial Dynamics of State Consolidation

The paper controls for several neighbor-level characteristics — military construction in foreign neighboring cities, exposure to warfare, and the share of fiscally centralized neighbors — and acknowledges that spillovers and SUTVA violations are genuine concerns. The intensive-margin analysis, which restricts the sample to the 39 eventually-treated territories, partially addresses this. But the fundamental issue remains: the paper’s outcomes are not independently generated across territories. One territory’s gain in cities is, by definition, another territory’s loss. A territory that becomes better defended through Chamber-funded military investment simultaneously alters the strategic environment — and hence the optimal strategies — of its neighbors. State consolidation in the Holy Roman Empire /ˌhoʊli ˈroʊmən ˈɛmpaɪər/ was a competitive, interactive process, not a collection of parallel, independent trajectories.

The most natural extension, therefore, is to move from treating neighbor-level variables as confounders to be controlled away, to modeling them as core components of an interactive structure. The same variable — “share of fiscally centralized neighbors” — plays a fundamentally different role depending on the theoretical framework. In the original paper, it is a nuisance variable that might bias the treatment effect estimate if left uncontrolled. In an interactive framework, it becomes a key explanatory variable: does a neighbor’s centralization raise or lower the returns to one’s own centralization? This is not merely a technical upgrade but a shift in theoretical perspective.

Such a framework could refine the paper’s causal interpretation in three specific ways. First, it could decompose the estimated treatment effect into the direct effect of a territory’s own Chamber adoption and the indirect effects operating through changes in the strategic environment — whether neighbors’ centralization creates threat effects (competitive pressure to also centralize), demonstration effects (learning about the benefits of centralization), or both. Second, it could sharpen the mechanism analysis. The paper’s military mechanism currently runs from centralization to increased military construction to better defense. But an interactive model would ask: does military construction increase because the territory is richer (a direct fiscal capacity effect), or because neighbors’ centralization has raised the external threat level, compelling a defensive response regardless of one’s own fiscal institutions? Disentangling these two channels would separate the “fiscal capacity” story from the “strategic environment” story. Third, it could clarify whether Chamber adoption across territories exhibits strategic complementarity (each territory’s adoption raises the returns to neighbors’ adoption, generating cascading centralization) or strategic substitutability (one territory’s adoption reduces neighbors’ incentives by making competition less winnable). This distinction matters for understanding the aggregate dynamics of state consolidation: complementarity would predict clustering and tipping points, while substitutability would predict stable asymmetries.

The data infrastructure for this extension already exists within the original paper. The authors have constructed annual territory-city control changes, neighbor identification for each city, foreign border information, neighbor military construction, and the share of fiscally centralized neighbors. What is needed is not a new data collection effort, but a reinterpretation of existing variables within an explicitly interactive theoretical and econometric framework — upgrading “controls” into “interaction structure.”
2. The Role of Human Capital and Bureaucratic Professionalization

The paper’s theoretical core is not simply that rulers created a new organization, but more specifically that the Chamber functioned as a knowledge-concentrating, collegially organized, specialized administrative layer that reduced the efficiency loss arising from the princely administration’s inability to handle fiscal complexity. This argument is explicitly grounded in Garicano /ɡɑːriˈtʃɑːnoʊ/ (2000)’s theory of knowledge hierarchies and Snowberg /ˈsnoʊbɜːrɡ/ and Ting /tɪŋ/ (2022)’s adaptation of that framework to state capacity. The key prediction of the Garicano model, however, is not merely that adding an organizational layer improves efficiency, but that the gains from hierarchical specialization depend critically on the problem-solving capacity of the agents staffing each layer. If Chamber officials were simply existing personnel reassigned under a new institutional label, the efficiency gains predicted by the knowledge hierarchy framework would not materialize. The implicit assumption in the paper — that the Chamber’s organizational form was sufficient to generate efficiency gains — therefore rests on an unstated premise: that personnel with the requisite skills in bookkeeping, auditing, document management, local-office monitoring, and centralized revenue coordination were available to staff these institutions.

This opens a precise research question: was Chamber adoption constrained not only by rulers’ incentive structures (as the paper models), but also by the supply of trained personnel capable of operating a knowledge hierarchy? In other words, did the organizational form matter, or did it matter only conditional on human capital availability? This question connects to a broader literature on state capacity that increasingly emphasizes bureaucratic quality and problem-solving ability, not just formal institutional authority. It also connects to the specifically European historical context in which universities, legal training, notarial traditions, and scribal expertise formed a critical infrastructure for administrative capacity — an infrastructure that varied across territories and over time.

The principal difficulty with this extension is empirical. The paper’s data advantages lie in adoption timing, territorial outcomes, marriage networks, and city-level spatial changes — none of which directly capture the human capital dimension. Pursuing this direction would require assembling Chamber personnel rosters, university matriculation records, legal training data, or office-holding biographies — a substantial archival undertaking that goes well beyond the original data collection. The theoretical motivation is strong and follows directly from the paper’s own Garicano /ɡɑːriˈtʃɑːnoʊ/ framing, but the feasibility barrier is real. This is an extension where the question is clearly posed by the paper’s own logic, but the empirical execution would constitute a largely independent research project.
3. Comparing Fiscal Centralization in Secular and Ecclesiastical Territories

Of the three extensions considered here, this one offers the most direct leverage for refining the paper’s core mechanism identification. The reason is that several of the paper’s key mechanisms and data structures are specific to secular territories in ways that are analytically consequential. The dynastic marriage network covers only secular rulers. The marriage success mechanism is inherently a hereditary-dynastic logic: daughters are married to build inheritance claims, which pay off when a lineage goes extinct. The “vanishing by dynastic extinction” outcome — treated as the exogenous, uncontrollable component of territorial survival — is also conceptualized around secular hereditary succession.

Ecclesiastical territories operated under fundamentally different institutional constraints. Their rulers — prince-bishops, prince-abbots — were elected, not hereditary. They could not form dynastic marriage alliances or transmit inheritance claims through offspring. This means that the dynastic-political mechanism channel identified in the paper — strategic marriages funded by Chamber revenues leading to inheritance claims leading to territorial expansion — was institutionally shut off in ecclesiastical territories. Ecclesiastical territories thus provide a setting in which the dynastic-political mechanism is closed by institutional design, while the administrative mechanism — better bookkeeping, more efficient revenue collection, improved auditing, pawn redemption, and military investment — can still operate. This approximates the logic of a natural experiment.

This creates an analytically powerful comparison. If both secular and ecclesiastical territories that adopted Chambers experienced improved survival, increased size, and greater compactness, the result would more strongly support the paper’s core interpretation: that the effect operates through pure fiscal-administrative centralization. These channels should function regardless of whether the ruler is a hereditary prince or an elected bishop. But if the consolidation effects are concentrated in secular territories, particularly through marriage-linked channels, then part of what the paper identifies as the “effect of fiscal centralization” is actually bundled with dynastic opportunity structures specific to hereditary rule. The treatment effect would then be partly a fiscal-administrative effect and partly a dynastic-political effect, and the paper’s current design cannot separate the two.

The secular-ecclesiastical comparison thus functions as a natural unbundling device. It allows the analyst to decompose the treatment effect into two layers: an administrative mechanism (bookkeeping, auditing, revenue coordination, pawn redemption, military funding) that should operate in both types of territories, and a dynastic-political mechanism (inheritance claims, marriage diplomacy, lineage continuity) that should operate only in secular territories. The paper already demonstrates that marriage is a mechanism, but it cannot tell us how much of the overall treatment effect flows through the marriage channel versus the purely administrative channel. The ecclesiastical territories provide a setting in which the marriage channel is institutionally closed, offering a cleaner estimate of the administrative component alone.

The feasibility of this extension is intermediate. On the outcome side, territorial survival, size, and compactness are not inherently limited to secular entities — ecclesiastical territories were territorial polities within the Holy Roman Empire and can be tracked using the same city-level data. The main difficulty lies on the mechanism side: the marriage network data and certain ruler-level codings were constructed specifically for secular rulers, so extending the analysis to ecclesiastical territories would require supplementary institutional coding. But a reduced-form comparison of consolidation outcomes — asking whether the Chamber effect differs between secular and ecclesiastical territories — is feasible without fully reconstructing the mechanism variables, and would already deliver substantial theoretical value.
zh: 1. 战略互动与国家整合的空间动态

本文控制了若干邻域层面的特征——外国邻近城市的军事建设、战争暴露以及财政集权化邻域的比例——并承认溢出效应和SUTVA违反是真实存在的问题。将样本限制在最终被处理的39个领地的密集边际分析部分回应了这一关切。但根本问题依然存在：本文的结果变量并非在各领地之间独立生成的。一个领地城市数量的增加，在定义上就是另一个领地的损失。一个通过财政室资助的军事投资而变得更易防守的领地，同时改变了其邻居的战略环境——从而也改变了邻居的最优策略。神圣罗马帝国 /ˌhoʊli ˈroʊmən ˈɛmpaɪər/ 的国家整合是一个竞争性的、互动的过程，而非一系列平行的、独立的轨迹。
因此，最自然的拓展方向是：不再把邻域层面的变量当作需要控制掉的混淆因素，而是将它们建模为互动结构的核心组成部分。同一个变量——“财政集权化邻域的比例”——在不同的理论框架下扮演着根本不同的角色。在原文中，它是一个如不控制就可能导致处理效应估计偏误的干扰变量。在互动框架中，它变成了一个核心解释变量：邻居的集权化是提高还是降低了本领地集权化的回报？这不仅仅是技术升级，而是理论视角的转换。

这样一个框架可以在三个具体方面精炼本文的因果解释。第一，它可以将估计的处理效应分解为领地自身建立财政室的直接效应和通过战略环境变化运作的间接效应——即邻居的集权化究竟产生了威胁效应（迫使本领地也集权化的竞争压力）、示范效应（关于集权化收益的学习），还是两者兼有。第二，它可以锐化机制分析。本文的军事机制目前是从集权化到更多军事建设到更好防御的链条。但互动模型会追问：军事建设的增加，是因为领地本身更富有（直接的财政能力效应），还是因为邻居的集权化提高了外部威胁水平，迫使本领地无论自身财政制度如何都必须加大防御投入？将这两个渠道拆分开来，就能把“财政能力”的故事与“战略环境变化”的故事区别开。第三，它可以澄清各领地的财政室建立之间呈现的是战略互补性（每个领地的建立提高了邻居建立的回报，产生级联式集权化）还是战略替代性（一个领地的建立降低了邻居的激励，因为竞争变得更难取胜）。这一区分对于理解国家整合的宏观动态至关重要：互补性预测的是集群化和临界点效应，而替代性预测的是稳定的不对称格局。
这一拓展所需的数据基础设施已经存在于原文之中。作者已经构建了年度领地-城市控制变更、每座城市的邻居识别、外国边界信息、邻居军事建设以及财政集权化邻域比例等变量。所需要的不是新的数据采集工作，而是在一个明确的互动理论和计量经济学框架中重新解读现有变量——将“控制变量”升级为“互动结构”。
2. 人力资本与官僚专业化的角色

本文的理论核心不仅仅是统治者创建了一个新组织，更具体地说，是财政室作为一个知识汇聚型的、合议制的、专业化的行政层级，降低了亲王行政机构无力处理财政复杂性所导致的效率损失。这一论证明确建立在加里卡诺 /ɡɑːriˈtʃɑːnoʊ/（2000）的知识层级理论以及斯诺伯格 /ˈsnoʊbɜːrɡ/ 和丁 /tɪŋ/（2022）将该框架应用于国家能力的工作之上。然而，加里卡诺模型的核心预测并非简单地“增加一个组织层级就能提高效率”，而是层级专业化的收益关键取决于充任该层级的行为主体的问题解决能力。如果财政室的官员不过是在新制度标签下被重新安排的原有人员，那么知识层级框架所预测的效率收益就不会实现。因此，本文的隐含假设——财政室的组织形式足以产生效率收益——实际上依赖于一个未被明确陈述的前提：具备簿记、审计、文档管理、地方办公室监督和集中化收入协调等必要技能的人员是可以获得的。

这就提出了一个精确的研究问题：财政室的建立是否不仅受到统治者激励结构的约束（如本文所建模的），还受到能够运作知识层级的受训人员供给的约束？换言之，组织形式本身是否重要，还是只有在人力资本可获得的条件下才重要？这个问题关联到一个更广泛的国家能力文献，该文献日益强调官僚质量和问题解决能力，而不仅仅是正式的制度权威。它还关联到一个具有欧洲历史特殊性的背景：大学、法学训练、公证人传统和书记官专业技能构成了行政能力形成的关键基础设施——而这一基础设施在不同领地之间和不同时期存在差异。

这一拓展方向的主要困难在于经验层面。本文的数据优势在于财政室建立时间、领地结果变量、婚姻网络和城市层面的空间变化——这些都不直接捕捉人力资本维度。推进这一方向需要收集财政室人员名册、大学注册记录、法学训练数据或职务履历——这是一项远超原文数据采集范围的大型档案工程。理论动机是强有力的，并直接源于本文自身的加里卡诺 /ɡɑːriˈtʃɑːnoʊ/ 框架，但可行性障碍是真实的。这是一个问题由本文自身的逻辑清晰提出、但经验实施将构成一个基本独立的研究项目的拓展方向。
3. 比较世俗领地与教会领地的财政集权化

在此处考虑的三个拓展方向中，这一个为精炼本文的核心机制识别提供了最直接的杠杆。原因在于，本文的若干关键机制和数据结构在分析上具有重要影响的方式上是特定于世俗领地的。王朝婚姻网络仅覆盖世俗统治者。婚姻成功机制在本质上是一种世袭-王朝逻辑：女儿通过婚姻建立继承权主张，当某一世系绝嗣时这些主张就会兑现。“因王朝绝嗣而消亡”这一结果变量——被视为领地存续中外生的、不可控的组成部分——同样是围绕世俗世袭继承来概念化的。

教会领地在根本不同的制度约束下运作。其统治者——亲王主教、亲王修道院长——是选举产生的，而非世袭的。他们不能结成王朝婚姻联盟，也不能通过后代传递继承权主张。这意味着本文所识别的王朝-政治机制渠道——由财政室收入资助的战略婚姻带来继承权主张进而导致领土扩张——在教会领地中被制度性地关闭了。教会领地因此提供了一个王朝-政治机制被制度设计所封闭、而行政机制——更好的簿记、更高效的收入征集、改进的审计、质押赎回和军事投资——仍然可以运作的场景。这近似于自然实验的逻辑。

这就创造了一个分析上有力的比较。如果建立了财政室的世俗领地和教会领地都经历了存续率提高、规模增大和紧凑度提升，那么该结果将更有力地支持本文的核心解释：效应通过纯粹的财政-行政集权化来运作。这些渠道无论统治者是世袭亲王还是选举产生的主教都应当发挥作用。但如果整合效应集中在世俗领地——特别是通过与婚姻相关的渠道——那么本文所识别的“财政集权化的效应”中就有一部分实际上是与世袭统治特有的王朝机会结构捆绑在一起的。处理效应将部分是财政-行政效应、部分是王朝-政治效应，而本文目前的研究设计无法将两者分离。

因此，世俗-教会比较发挥着一种自然的“解捆绑”（unbundling）功能。它使分析者能够将处理效应分解为两个层次：一个是行政机制（簿记、审计、收入协调、质押赎回、军事资助），它应当在两类领地中都发挥作用；另一个是王朝-政治机制（继承权主张、婚姻外交、世系延续），它应当仅在世俗领地中发挥作用。本文已经证明婚姻是一个机制，但它无法告诉我们整体处理效应中有多少通过婚姻渠道流动、有多少通过纯行政渠道流动。教会领地提供了一个婚姻渠道被制度性关闭的场景，从而能够更干净地估计仅行政机制的效应。

这一拓展方向的可行性处于中等水平。在结果变量方面，领地存续、规模和紧凑度在本质上并不限于世俗实体——教会领地是神圣罗马帝国内的领土政治实体，可以使用同样的城市层面数据进行追踪。主要困难在于机制变量方面：婚姻网络数据和某些统治者层面的编码是专门为世俗统治者构建的，因此将分析拓展到教会领地需要补充性的制度编码。但一个简约式的整合结果比较——考察财政室效应在世俗领地和教会领地之间是否存在差异——在不完全重建机制变量的情况下是可行的，且已经能够提供实质性的理论价值。

en: “State-Building and Rebellion in the Run-Up to the French Revolution”

This article examines how the expansion of the French monarchy’s horse-post relay network (poste aux chevaux) during the eighteenth century triggered localized rebellions across the French territory in the decades leading up to the Revolution of 1789. The paper uses original parish-level data and a staggered difference-in-differences design to establish a causal link between state communication infrastructure and popular unrest (p. 1).
zh: “法国大革命前夕的国家建设与叛乱”
本文考察了十八世纪法国君主制的驿站网络（poste aux chevaux）的扩张如何在1789年大革命前夕的数十年间在法国全境引发了地方性叛乱。文章使用了原始的教区级数据和交错双重差分设计，以建立国家通信基础设施与民众骚乱之间的因果联系（第1页）。

en: Michael Albertus /ˈmaɪkəl ælˈbɜːrtəs/, Professor, Department of Political Science, University of Chicago /ʃɪˈkɑːɡoʊ/, United States.
Victor Gay /ˈvɪktər ɡeɪ/, Assistant Professor of Economics, Department of Social and Behavioral Sciences, Toulouse /tuːˈluːz/ School of Economics, France.

Albertus is known for his work on authoritarianism, land reform, and property rights. Gay is an economic historian specializing in French historical data and quantitative methods. Their collaboration brings together political science and economic history to examine a canonical case of state formation.
zh: Albertus以其在威权主义、土地改革和产权方面的研究著称。Gay是一位专门研究法国历史数据和定量方法的经济史学家。他们的合作将政治学与经济史结合在一起，考察了一个国家形成的经典案例。

en: The article is forthcoming in the American Political Science Review (APSR), 2025. It was received on October 24, 2024, revised on February 21, 2025, and accepted on November 12, 2025 (p. 1).
zh: 本文即将发表于《美国政治学评论》（APSR），2025年。稿件于2024年10月24日收到，2025年2月21日修订，2025年11月12日接受（第1页）。

en: The main dependent variable is the number of rebellions per parish per decade in eighteenth-century France. The data come from the Jean Nicolas /ʒɑ̃ nikɔˈla/ survey (Nicolas 1985; 2002), which documents 8,516 rebellions across France between 1661 and 1789. The authors focus on the 6,000 rebellions that occurred in 3,172 parishes within the kingdom’s boundaries between 1714 and 1789 (pp. 9–10). The survey sets a low threshold for inclusion: any uprising that lasted only a few hours or involved as few as four individuals qualifies. This makes the survey an unusually comprehensive “thermometer” of popular discontent (p. 10). In robustness checks, the authors also use an indicator variable for whether any rebellion occurred in a parish-decade, and they disaggregate by rebellion intensity (weak: 4–10 participants; moderate: 11–50; strong: 50+), by targets of rebellion (state authorities vs. non-state authorities, taxation vs. military vs. judiciary), and by the social identity of protagonists (notables, laborers, craftsmen, etc.) (pp. 9–10, Table 1).
zh: 主要因变量是十八世纪法国每个教区每十年的叛乱次数。数据来源于让·尼古拉 /ʒɑ̃ nikɔˈla/ 的调查（Nicolas 1985; 2002），该调查记录了1661年至1789年间法国全境8,516次叛乱。作者聚焦于1714年至1789年间王国境内3,172个教区中发生的6,000次叛乱（第9–10页）。调查设定了较低的纳入门槛：任何仅持续数小时或涉及少至四人的起义均符合条件。这使得该调查成为衡量民众不满的异常全面的“温度计”（第10页）。在稳健性检验中，作者还使用了教区-十年中是否发生过任何叛乱的指示变量，并按叛乱强度（弱：4–10人；中等：11–50人；强：50人以上）、叛乱对象（国家当局vs.非国家当局，税收vs.军事vs.司法）以及参与者社会身份（地方显贵、劳工、工匠等）进行分类（第9–10页，表1）。

en: The main independent variable is the presence of a horse-post relay in a parish in a given decade. This is a binary, non-absorbing treatment: a parish can gain a relay, lose it, regain it, etc. The authors construct an original historical GIS of the horse-post relay network based on administrative sources — specifically, the Liste générale des postes de France, an official publication printed annually from 1708 onward that listed all relays and distances between them. The authors digitized one edition per decade from the 1710s through the 1790s. The network grew from 841 relays spanning 11,000 km of postal roads in 1714 to 1,403 relays covering 24,000 km in 1790 — essentially doubling in size over the period (pp. 7–8, Figure 3). The expansion was not linear: it involved the creation of 1,001 relays and the retirement of 439 (p. 9). Over the eighteenth century, the network shifted from a center-periphery configuration driven by geopolitical and military concerns toward an internal densification aimed at projecting monarchic authority into the kingdom’s interior (pp. 7–8).
zh: 主要自变量是某教区在某个十年期内是否存在驿站。这是一个二元的、非吸收性的处理变量：一个教区可以获得一个驿站，也可以失去它，然后再次获得，等等。作者基于行政来源——具体而言是1708年起每年印刷的官方出版物《法国驿站总表》（Liste générale des postes de France）——构建了驿站网络的原始历史GIS。作者将1710年代至1790年代每十年的一个版本进行了数字化。网络从1714年的841个驿站、11,000公里邮路增长到1790年的1,403个驿站、24,000公里邮路——在此期间基本上翻了一番（第7–8页，图3）。扩张并非线性的：共涉及1,001个驿站的新建和439个驿站的撤销（第9页）。在十八世纪期间，网络从由地缘政治和军事考量驱动的中心-外围格局，转向以将君主权威投射到王国内陆为目标的内部加密（第7–8页）。

en: The paper draws on a rich body of state formation theory. The state is conceptualized in Weberian terms as an entity seeking a monopoly over violence and the centralized administration of territory (Weber 1968), but the emphasis throughout the paper is on the process of achieving that monopoly — that is, on state-building rather than state strength. The authors engage directly with Michael Mann’s /maɪkəl mæn/ (1984) concept of infrastructural power — the capacity of the central state to penetrate its territories and logistically implement its decisions — as distinct from despotic power, the ability to impose mandates over society without negotiation. The horse-post relay network is treated as a concrete manifestation of the state’s attempt to build infrastructural power: it enhanced the monarchy’s informational capacity, allowed coordination among tax collectors, intendants, and military officials, and extended the state’s administrative reach into remote provinces (pp. 2–3). The authors also draw on Charles Tilly’s /tʃɑːrlz ˈtɪli/ (1990) bellicist framework, noting that the initial configuration of the horse-post network reflected strategic military concerns vis-à-vis neighboring powers (Spain, England, the Holy Roman Empire), while eighteenth-century expansion was driven by internal consolidation (pp. 7–8). The state is further conceptualized through Norbert Elias’s /ˈnɔːrbɛrt ˈeɪliəs/ ([1939] 1994) account of state centralization as a long, centuries-spanning process of absorbing feudal capacities and creating “civilizing” norms of state control over violence (p. 2). Crucially, the paper insists on distinguishing the process of state-building from the outcome of state strength: while greater state capacity may ultimately support political stability and order, the process of building that capacity can be disruptive, contested, and generative of popular resistance (pp. 2–3, 25). This distinction — between the long-run equilibrium effect of state capacity and its transitional, destabilizing dynamics — is the paper’s key conceptual contribution to state formation theory. The measurement of stateness is thus operationalized through a concrete, observable indicator of state penetration: the physical presence of a horse-post relay in a parish, which represented an attended building with stables and lodging quarters, staffed by uniformed postillons and managed by a state-appointed postmaster who served as both a relay operator and an intelligence agent for the monarchy (pp. 4–5).
zh: 本文借鉴了丰富的国家形成理论体系。国家按韦伯式的定义被概念化为一个寻求在其领土内垄断暴力并集中管理领土的实体（Weber 1968），但全文的重点在于实现这一垄断的过程——即国家建设而非国家实力。作者直接运用了迈克尔·曼 /maɪkəl mæn/（1984）的基础性权力概念——中央国家渗透其领土并在后勤上执行其决策的能力——以区别于专制权力，即无需谈判即可将命令强加于社会的能力。驿站网络被视为国家试图建设基础性权力的具体体现：它增强了君主制的信息能力，允许税收官员、省督（intendants）和军事官员之间的协调，并将国家的行政触角延伸到偏远省份（第2–3页）。作者还借鉴了查尔斯·蒂利 /tʃɑːrlz ˈtɪli/（1990）的战争主义框架，指出驿站网络的初始配置反映了相对于邻国（西班牙、英格兰、神圣罗马帝国）的战略军事考量，而十八世纪的扩张则由内部整合所驱动（第7–8页）。国家还通过诺贝特·埃利亚斯 /ˈnɔːrbɛrt ˈeɪliəs/（[1939] 1994）的叙述被进一步概念化，他将国家集中化视为一个长达数世纪的吸收封建能力并创造国家暴力控制“文明化”规范的过程（第2页）。至关重要的是，本文坚持区分国家建设的过程与国家实力的结果：虽然更大的国家能力最终可能支持政治稳定和秩序，但建设这种能力的过程可能是破坏性的、充满争议的，并会引发民众抵抗（第2–3页，第25页）。这一区分——长期均衡效应与过渡性、不稳定动态之间的区分——是本文对国家形成理论的关键概念贡献。因此，国家性通过一个具体的、可观察的国家渗透指标来操作化：教区中驿站的物理存在，这代表着一座有人值守的建筑物，配有马厩和住宿区，由穿制服的驿卒运营，并由国家任命的驿站长管理，驿站长同时充当驿站运营者和为君主服务的情报人员（第4–5页）。

en: The core argument is that the expansion of the state’s communication infrastructure — specifically, the horse-post relay network — increased local rebellion in eighteenth-century France. This finding is attributed primarily to the material consequences of state centralization: the establishment of new horse-post relays brought increased state presence that disrupted local social equilibria in several concrete ways. The state agents who followed or were facilitated by the horse post — tax collectors, military recruiters, judicial officials, and police (maréchaussée /maʁeʃoˈse/) — became targets of popular ire for their coercive activities: forced conscription, tax enforcement, monopolization of postal roads, and inflexible enforcement of royal laws perceived as unfair (pp. 1–2, 5–6). The paper thus presents an argument about the contested nature of state-building itself: while greater state capacity may ultimately support stability, the process of building it generates backlash, especially when the state has enough power to intrude into local life but not enough coercive capacity or legitimacy to deter resistance (p. 3). The argument further proposes that this backlash varied across the territory in accordance with pre-existing levels of state and administrative fragmentation: areas near fiscal boundaries (gabelles /ɡaˈbɛl/ frontiers) and regions historically more remote from direct monarchic control (pays d'États and pays d'Imposition vs. pays d'Élection) showed disproportionately stronger responses (pp. 20–21, Table 5). This finding connects the paper to Joel Migdal’s /dʒoʊˈɛl ˈmɪɡdæl/ (1988) argument about strong societies and weak states: pre-existing regional divisions and competing authorities conditioned the state’s ability to penetrate and order society in a consistent fashion (p. 3).
zh: 核心论点是国家通信基础设施的扩张——具体而言是驿站网络——增加了十八世纪法国的地方叛乱。这一发现主要归因于国家集中化的物质后果：新驿站的建立带来了更多的国家存在，以多种具体方式打破了地方社会均衡。跟随或受驿站便利的国家代理人——税收官员、军事征兵官、司法官员和宪兵（maréchaussée /maʁeʃoˈse/）——因其强制性活动而成为民众愤怒的对象：强制征兵、税收执法、邮政道路的垄断，以及被认为不公正的王室法律的僵硬执行（第1–2、5–6页）。因此，本文提出了一个关于国家建设本身的争议性质的论点：虽然更大的国家能力最终可能支持稳定，但建设它的过程会引发反弹，尤其是当国家有足够的权力侵入地方生活但没有足够的强制能力或合法性来威慑抵抗时（第3页）。论点还进一步提出，这种反弹在全境内随着先前存在的国家和行政碎片化程度而变化：靠近财政边界（盐税区 /ɡaˈbɛl/ 边境）的地区以及历史上更远离直接君主控制的地区（三级会议省和税征省vs.选举省）表现出不成比例的更强烈反应（第20–21页，表5）。这一发现将本文与乔尔·米格达尔 /dʒoʊˈɛl ˈmɪɡdæl/（1988）关于强社会与弱国家的论点联系起来：先前存在的地区分裂和竞争性权威影响了国家以一致方式渗透和管理社会的能力（第3页）。

en: The authors propose that the link between new horse-post relays and rebellion operates primarily through the material consequences of state penetration, rather than through information diffusion or collective action facilitation. They identify several specific channels:

Mechanism 1: Tax enforcement. Improved speed and reliability of state communication facilitated stricter tax enforcement, crystallizing popular ire against state tax obligations. The horse-post network allowed central officials, tax farmers (fermiers généraux), and provincial intendants to coordinate swiftly on tax collection and enforcement, including efforts to suppress salt smuggling (faux-saunage) across the kingdom’s disparate salt-tax zones (gabelles) (p. 5). Rebellions against taxation account for about one-fourth of the total effect (p. 19, Table 4, column 3).

Mechanism 2: Military conscription. The network facilitated the transmission of military recruitment orders and enabled information gathering on draft-eligible men. The authors provide a vivid example from 1781 in Mirande /miˈʁɑ̃d/, in the southwestern généralité of Auch /oʃ/, where draft-eligible peasants confronted military officials at a conscription draw, shouting “They should all be killed!” (p. 6). Rebellions against the military account for about one-fifth of the total effect (p. 20, Table 4, column 4).

Mechanism 3: Judicial enforcement. Stricter enforcement of royal laws and court orders — facilitated by faster communication — disrupted local social balance and generated grievances. Rebellions against the judiciary account for about one-fourth of the total effect (p. 20, Table 4, column 5).

Mechanism 4: Crowding out of private interests. The monopolies associated with the horse post — monopoly on horse rentals along postal roads, monopoly on galloping at night, priority access to hay and animal fodder — disrupted competing private businesses and generated resentment among local notables, horse renters, farmers, and innkeepers situated off postal roads (pp. 5–6). Rebellions led by notables account for about one-fifth of the total effect (p. 20, Table 4, column 6). The authors provide an example from 1775 in Chauny /ʃoˈni/, Picardy /ˈpɪkərdi/, where a crowd attacked and ransacked a horse-post relay on rumors that the postmaster had stockpiled wheat during a local shortage (pp. 6–7).

Mechanism 5: Gathering effects. The visible new state presence — large buildings with stables, uniformed postillons, and royal-marked horses — may have drawn people from surrounding parishes to express grievances at these new symbols of state authority. The authors find evidence of a “gathering effect”: rebellions decreased in parishes within 5–10 km of a new relay, while increasing in the host parish itself. However, the net effect remains positive and is about twice the magnitude of the cumulated spillover effects (pp. 21–22, Figure 8).

Mechanism 6: Pre-existing fragmentation as a moderator. The authors find that the rebellion response to new relays was disproportionately stronger in areas of pre-existing fiscal and administrative fragmentation: near gabelles frontiers and in pays d'États/d'Imposition relative to pays d'Élection (pp. 20–21, Table 5).

The authors explicitly test and reject several alternative mechanisms: (i) disruption of traditional social hierarchies — they find no effect on rebellions against the Church, nobility, or seigneurial authorities (p. 22); (ii) war zones and wartime — the effect is robust to excluding border regions and wartime rebellions (pp. 22–23); (iii) information diffusion via the contemporaneous letter-post system — controlling for letter-post offices yields virtually identical estimates (p. 23); (iv) transit infrastructure — the horse post was built atop existing roads and the road network was managed independently by the Bridges and Roads (Ponts et Chaussées) administration (pp. 23–24); (v) policing by maréchaussée brigades — the spatial development of brigades followed a different logic, and controlling for brigade presence does not change the results (pp. 24–25).
Alternative explanations the authors examine.

The paper also works to rule out several rival accounts. The authors find no comparable effect on rebellions against non-state authorities such as the Church, nobility, seigneurial, or municipal authorities, which weakens the idea that relay expansion mainly disrupted traditional hierarchies in a broad social sense. They also show that the results are robust to excluding border regions and wartime rebellions, making it less likely that the estimates are driven simply by war exposure. In addition, controlling for the letter-post system yields very similar estimates, which weakens the argument that the main effect operates through broader information diffusion. Finally, the authors argue that the results are unlikely to be driven by transportation infrastructure per se or by the deployment of maréchaussée brigades, since the horse-post network was built on preexisting roads, roads were administered separately by the Ponts et Chaussées, and brigade expansion followed a different spatial logic. Still, the authors do not claim to have disproven in all settings the possibility that communication infrastructure may facilitate collective action; rather, they argue that in this case, the evidence points more strongly toward the material consequences of state centralization.
zh: 作者提出，新驿站与叛乱之间的联系主要通过国家渗透的物质后果运作，而非通过信息传播或集体行动的促进。他们识别了几个具体渠道：
机制一：税收执法。 国家通信速度和可靠性的提高促进了更严格的税收执法，使民众对国家税收义务的愤怒具体化。驿站网络使中央官员、包税商（fermiers généraux）和省督能够迅速协调税收征管和执法，包括打击全国分散盐税区（gabelles）之间的走私活动（faux-saunage）（第5页）。针对税收的叛乱约占总效应的四分之一（第19页，表4，第3列）。
机制二：军事征兵。 网络促进了军事征兵令的传达，并使收集适龄男性信息成为可能。作者提供了一个来自1781年西南部Auch /oʃ/ 总督区Mirande /miˈʁɑ̃d/ 的生动案例，在那里适龄农民在征兵抽签现场面对军官，高喊“应该把他们全部杀掉！”（第6页）。针对军事的叛乱约占总效应的五分之一（第20页，表4，第4列）。
机制三：司法执法。 更快的通信促进了对王室法律和法院裁决的更严格执行，打破了地方社会平衡并产生了不满。针对司法的叛乱约占总效应的四分之一（第20页，表4，第5列）。

机制四：排挤私人利益。 与驿站相关的垄断——邮政道路上的马匹租赁垄断、夜间疾驰的垄断、优先获取干草和饲料——扰乱了竞争性的私人企业，在地方显贵、马匹租赁商、农民和不在邮路上的旅店主中产生了怨恨（第5–6页）。由显贵领导的叛乱约占总效应的五分之一（第20页，表4，第6列）。作者提供了一个来自1775年皮卡第 /ˈpɪkərdi/ 的Chauny /ʃoˈni/ 的案例，一群人在传闻驿站长在当地粮食短缺期间囤积小麦后袭击并洗劫了当地驿站（第6–7页）。
机制五：聚集效应。 可见的新国家存在——大型建筑物、穿制服的驿卒、带有王室标记的马匹——可能吸引了周围教区的人们到这些新的国家权威象征处表达不满。作者发现了“聚集效应”的证据：在距新驿站5–10公里范围内的教区叛乱减少了，而在驿站所在教区本身则增加了。然而，净效应仍为正值，约为累积溢出效应的两倍（第21–22页，图8）。
机制六：先前存在的碎片化作为调节变量。 作者发现，在先前存在财政和行政碎片化的地区，叛乱对新驿站的反应不成比例地更强烈：在盐税区边境附近以及三级会议省/税征省（相对于选举省）（第20–21页，表5）。

作者明确检验并否定了几个替代机制：(i) 传统社会等级的瓦解——他们发现对针对教会、贵族或领主权威的叛乱没有影响（第22页）；(ii) 战区和战时——在排除边境地区和战时叛乱后效果稳健（第22–23页）；(iii) 通过同期信件邮政系统的信息扩散——控制信件邮局后估计值几乎不变（第23页）；(iv) 交通基础设施——驿站建在已有道路之上，道路网络由桥梁与道路管理局（Ponts et Chaussées）独立管理（第23–24页）；(v) 宪兵巡逻——宪兵旅的空间发展遵循不同的逻辑，控制宪兵存在不改变结果（第24–25页）。

en: The analysis dataset is a panel of approximately 35,000 parishes of the kingdom of France as of 1789 (excluding Corsica /ˈkɔːrsɪkə/), spanning eight decades from the 1710s to the 1780s — a total of 282,288 parish-decade observations. Parishes were the smallest units of administration in Ancien Régime France, and no prior study had analyzed this period at such a granular level (p. 7).

The horse-post relay network data are an original historical GIS constructed from the Liste générale des postes de France. The authors digitized one edition per decade from 1714 to 1790, extracting all segments between relays and matching each location to the corresponding parish (pp. 7–8). The data source is administrative and designed for state messengers, which makes it highly reliable.

The rebellion data come from the Jean Nicolas /ʒɑ̃ nikɔˈla/ survey (Nicolas 1985; 2002), compiled from extensive archival research during the 1980s–1990s. The authors digitized all 40,000 original coding sheets of the survey and constructed a comprehensive database of the 6,000 rebellions that took place in 3,172 parishes between 1714 and 1789. For each event, they recorded the date, location, motives, size, and characteristics of participants (pp. 9–10).

Additional data include: (i) parish-level decade-average wheat prices from Ridolfi /rɪˈdɒlfi/ (2019), used to control for local economic conditions; (ii) population from the 1793 census (the first census conducted in France); (iii) road connectivity from Cassini’s /kæˈsiːni/ maps published in 1756–90; (iv) whether a parish served as an administrative seat; (v) the type of gabelle taxation regime; (vi) a historical GIS of both the letter-post system and maréchaussée brigades, constructed by the authors for the alternative-mechanisms analysis (p. 12).

The unit of analysis is the parish-decade. The key administrative divisions used in the empirical strategy are cantons (over 4,500, with a median radius of 6 km and 7 parishes each) and subdélégations /sybdeleɡaˈsjɔ̃/ (nearly 700, with a median radius of 15 km and 39 parishes each) (pp. 11–12).
